summary:
The Ghaznavid Empire (977–1186 AD) was a Persianate Turkic sultanate centered at Ghazni in modern Afghanistan, founded by Sebuktigin — a former Turkish slave-soldier in the [[event:2191|Samanid Empire]] — who established an independent dynasty after his master's power weakened. The Ghaznavids expanded rapidly under Sebuktigin's son [[event:2930|Mahmud of Ghazni]], becoming the dominant power of the eastern Islamic world and the first major Islamic state to style itself a sultanate, claiming authority over an empire stretching from the Hindu Kush to the Persian Gulf.

The Ghaznavid court at Ghazni became one of the most brilliant cultural centers of the Islamic world despite its origin as a frontier military state. Mahmud was a passionate patron of Persian literature, attracting poets including the great Ferdowsi, whose epic Shahnameh (Book of Kings) — commissioned by Mahmud, though the relationship between poet and patron eventually soured — became the foundational text of Persian literary identity. The philosopher-scientist [[event:3615|al-Biruni]] accompanied Mahmud's India campaigns and produced his encyclopedic 'Kitab ul-Hind' (Book of India), the most sophisticated cross-cultural study of Indian civilization produced in the medieval Islamic world. The Ghaznavid Empire's military culture, administrative practices, and Persian cultural orientation all influenced the subsequent Ghurid and Delhi Sultanate states that would dominate South Asia, making Ghazni's brief period of imperial glory disproportionately influential on the long-term history of Islamic South Asia.
